does: This paper introduces EPO-Safe, a framework that enables language model agents to discover safety-aligned behavioral specifications through iterative interaction with extremely sparse feedback. The core method has an LLM generate action plans, receive single-bit binary danger signals indicating unsafe actions, and refine a natural language specification through self-reflection cycles. Evaluated on five AI Safety Gridworlds and five text-based scenario analogs designed to separate visible rewards from true safety objectives, the framework discovers safe behavior within 1-2 rounds (5-15 episodes), generating human-readable specifications that correctly identify hazards without ever observing the underlying reward function—only accessing the impoverished one-bit safety signal per timestep.
connects: This work extends the emerging literature on LLM agents learning from feedback, building particularly on standard reflection methods that have shown LLMs can improve reasoning from rich textual signals like compiler errors or detailed environment descriptions. It directly addresses a gap in agent alignment research by examining whether the sparse binary feedback regime—far more realistic for genuine safety-critical domains—suffices for specification discovery. The paper connects to AI Safety Gridworlds as a benchmark and the broader problem of reward misspecification where observed rewards diverge from true objectives, drawing on work in specification gaming and value alignment that motivates learning directly from safety constraints rather than reward signals alone.
enables: This framework opens several directions for practical safe AI deployment. It suggests that agents can discover safety properties from minimal supervision, which could reduce the engineering burden of specifying complex safety objectives before deployment. The approach points toward agents that learn hidden safety constraints through exploration, applicable to scenarios where true reward functions are expensive or dangerous to reveal during training. Future work could extend this to continuous or higher-dimensional feedback signals, investigate how performance scales with environment complexity, and explore whether agents can jointly discover multiple competing safety specifications or handle specification uncertainty in real-world deployment contexts.
